title: Time in New Zealand

infobox.value: is divided by law into two [standard](/wiki/Standard_time "Standard time") [time zones](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone"). The main islands use New Zealand Standard Time (NZST), 12 hours in advance of [Coordinated Universal Time](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") (UTC) / [military](/wiki/Military_time_zone "Military time zone") M (Mike),[[1]](#cite_note-Military_Time_Zone_map-1) while the outlying [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") use [Chatham Standard Time](/wiki/Chatham_Standard_Time_Zone "Chatham Standard Time Zone") (CHAST), 12 hours 45 minutes in advance of UTC / military M^ (Mike-Three).[[1]](#cite_note-Military_Time_Zone_map-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

"NZT" redirects here. For other uses, see [NZT (disambiguation)](/wiki/NZT_(disambiguation) "NZT (disambiguation)").

| Time zone | Standard time | Daylight time |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") | [UTC+12:00](/wiki/UTC%2B12:00 "UTC+12:00") | [UTC+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") |
| [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") | [UTC+12:45](/wiki/UTC%2B12:45 "UTC+12:45") | [UTC+13:45](/wiki/UTC%2B13:45 "UTC+13:45") |

**Time in New Zealand** is divided by law into two [standard](/wiki/Standard_time "Standard time") [time zones](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone"). The main islands use New Zealand Standard Time (NZST), 12 hours in advance of [Coordinated Universal Time](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") (UTC) / [military](/wiki/Military_time_zone "Military time zone") M (Mike),[[1]](#cite_note-Military_Time_Zone_map-1) while the outlying [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") use [Chatham Standard Time](/wiki/Chatham_Standard_Time_Zone "Chatham Standard Time Zone") (CHAST), 12 hours 45 minutes in advance of UTC / military M^ (Mike-Three).[[1]](#cite_note-Military_Time_Zone_map-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

During summer months – from the last Sunday in September until the first Sunday in April – [daylight saving time](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time") is observed and clocks are advanced one hour. New Zealand Daylight Time (NZDT) is 13 hours ahead of UTC, and Chatham Daylight Time (CHADT) 13 hours 45 minutes ahead.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

New Zealand's associated states – the [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands") and [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue") – and the dependent territory of [Tokelau](/wiki/Tokelau "Tokelau") use several different time zones at their own discretion.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Time_in_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

On 2 November 1868, New Zealand officially adopted a standard time to be observed nationally, and was the first country to do so, about fifteen years before any other. Chatham Island was 45 minutes ahead of mainland New Zealand [twelve hours and fifteen minutes ahead of Coordinated Universal Time].[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) It was based on [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") 172° 30′ East of [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich "Greenwich"), 11+1⁄2 hours ahead of [Greenwich Mean Time](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time") (GMT).[[6]](#cite_note-6) This standard was known as New Zealand Mean Time (NZMT).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 1941, during the Second World War, clocks were advanced half an hour, to reduce electric power use[[8]](#cite_note-8) making New Zealand 12 hours ahead of GMT. This change was made permanent from 1946 by the *Standard Time Act 1945*,[[9]](#cite_note-DIA-9) at which the time at the 180°E meridian was made the basis for New Zealand Time.[[10]](#cite_note-10) NZST remained half an hour ahead of NZMT, and the [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") 45 minutes ahead of NZST.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Atomic_clock.jpg/250px-Atomic_clock.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atomic_clock.jpg)

The use of atomic clocks, which can measure time extremely accurately, led to the adoption of UTC. This was incorporated into New Zealand law in 1974.

In the late 1940s the [atomic clock](/wiki/Atomic_clock "Atomic clock") was developed and several laboratories began atomic time scales. A new time scale known as Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) was adopted internationally in 1972.[[11]](#cite_note-BIPM-11) This was based on the readings of atomic clocks, updated periodically in accordance with time variations in the Earth's rotation by the addition or deletion of seconds (called [leap seconds](/wiki/Leap_second "Leap second")). The *Time Act 1974* defines New Zealand Standard Time as 12 hours in advance of UTC.[[12]](#cite_note-1974Act-12)

In 2011, the New Zealand dependency of Tokelau moved its time zone forward by 24 hours, by skipping 30 December to be in the [UTC+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") time zone, the same zone as New Zealand daylight saving.[[13]](#cite_note-RNZI_63444-13)

## Daylight saving time (DST)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Time_in_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Daylight saving time (DST)")]

Starting in 1909, a member of Parliament, [Sir Thomas Kay Sidey](/wiki/Thomas_Sidey "Thomas Sidey") annually put forward a bill to advance the clocks an hour from September to the following March and the *Summer Time Act 1927* succeeded.[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 1927, clocks were advanced by an hour from first Sunday in November to the first Sunday in March. This proved unpopular, so the *Summer Time Act 1928* revised this to a *half*-hour shift from 14 October 1928 (second Sunday) to 17 March 1929 (third Sunday), then the *Summer Time Act 1929* permanently fixed this half-hour shift to run from the second Sunday in October to the third Sunday in March every year. In 1933, the period was extended from the last Sunday in September to the last Sunday in April. This continued until the Second World War, when emergency regulations in 1941 extended daylight saving to cover the whole year with annual re-applications until the *Standard Time Act 1945* made the abandonment of NZMT permanent in 1946, so that 180° becomes the base longitude and what was called NZ Summer Time (NZST) became NZ Standard Time.

The *Time Act 1974* empowered the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand") to declare by [Order in Council](/wiki/Order_in_Council "Order in Council") a period when daylight saving time is to be observed.[[12]](#cite_note-1974Act-12) This was a one-hour shift (on top of the earlier half-hour shift) from the first Sunday in November to the last Sunday in February, promptly changed the next year by The New Zealand Time Order 1975 which fixed the period of observance from the last Sunday in October to the first Sunday in March.

In 1985 a comprehensive survey was undertaken by the Department of Internal Affairs. Public attitudes towards NZDT (New Zealand Daylight Time) and its effects on work, recreation and particular groups of people in society were surveyed. The survey indicated that 76.2% of the population wanted NZDT either continued or extended.[[15]](#cite_note-Pearce2017-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Abaconda_leigh_farm.jpg/250px-Abaconda_leigh_farm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abaconda_leigh_farm.jpg)

The strongest opposition to DST has traditionally come from dairy farmers[[16]](#cite_note-ararua_time-16) due to disrupted schedules and shorter working times.

The survey also concluded that opinion on the topic differed little between sexes, and that support for NZDT was generally higher in [urban centres](/wiki/Urban_areas_of_New_Zealand "Urban areas of New Zealand"). Daylight saving was famously opposed in the small [Northland](/wiki/Northland_Region "Northland Region") dairy farming community of Ararua,[[16]](#cite_note-ararua_time-16) which refused to adjust its clocks for some years. Support for shortening or abolishing NZDT was always in the minority in the areas surveyed.

As a consequence of the survey and further feedback from the public, in 1988 the Minister of Internal Affairs arranged for a trial period of extended NZDT to be held from the second Sunday in October 1989 to the third Sunday in March 1990. The Minister invited the public to write to him with their views on the five-week extension.[[15]](#cite_note-Pearce2017-15)

The *Daylight Time Order 1990* declared that NZDT would run from 02:00 NZST on the first Sunday in October to 03:00 NZDT on the third Sunday in March.[[9]](#cite_note-DIA-9)

On 30 April 2007 the government announced that it had extended the daylight saving period from 24 to 27 weeks.[[17]](#cite_note-17) From September 2007, daylight saving is now observed from the last Sunday in September until the first Sunday in April. From 30 April 2007, DST begins at 02:00 NZST on the last Sunday in September each year, and ends at 03:00 NZDT (or 02:00 NZST as defined in the 1974 Act) on the first Sunday in April.

New Zealand time, including DST, is used by several [Antarctic](/wiki/Antarctic "Antarctic") bases that are supplied from New Zealand. This results in the oddity that the [Amundsen–Scott South Pole Station](/wiki/Amundsen%E2%80%93Scott_South_Pole_Station "Amundsen–Scott South Pole Station") sets its clocks an hour further ahead during the southern summer, when the sun is constantly above the horizon, than in the southern winter, when the sun is constantly below the horizon. The extreme geographic position of the base means that no possible adjustment of the daily activity cycle can have any effect on the amount of sunlight received during those activities. However, the arrangement presumably makes real time communications with New Zealand more practical, particularly in dealing with offices.

The other countries in the [Realm of New Zealand](/wiki/Realm_of_New_Zealand "Realm of New Zealand") – [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands"), [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue"), and [Tokelau](/wiki/Tokelau "Tokelau") – do not maintain DST. Two of them are on the other side of the [International Date Line](/wiki/International_Date_Line "International Date Line") and have 22–24 hours time difference to New Zealand.

The following table lists recent and near future starting and ending dates of daylight saving time in the main islands of New Zealand:

| Year | End | Start |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2022 | 3 April | 25 September |
| 2023 | 2 April | 24 September |
| 2024 | 7 April | 29 September |
| 2025 | 6 April | 28 September |
| 2026 | 5 April | 27 September |
| 2027 | 4 April | 26 September |

## Standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Time_in_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Standards")]

New Zealand standard time is maintained by the Measurement Standards Laboratory (MSL), part of [New Zealand Government](/wiki/New_Zealand_Government "New Zealand Government"). New Zealand standard time is based on Coordinated Universal Time (UTC). UTC is kept within 200 nanoseconds of the international atomic time scale maintained by the [International Bureau of Weights and Measures](/wiki/International_Bureau_of_Weights_and_Measures "International Bureau of Weights and Measures") in Paris.[[11]](#cite_note-BIPM-11)

It is disseminated by various means, including time pips broadcast on [Radio New Zealand](/wiki/Radio_New_Zealand "Radio New Zealand"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) [speaking clock](/wiki/Speaking_clock "Speaking clock") and [Network Time Protocol](/wiki/Network_Time_Protocol "Network Time Protocol").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Dependencies and associated states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Time_in_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dependencies and associated states")]

| Time zone | Standard time | Daylight time |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Tokelau](/wiki/Tokelau "Tokelau") | [UTC+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") | |
| [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands") | [UTC−10:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9210:00 "UTC−10:00") | |
| [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue") | [UTC−11:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9211:00 "UTC−11:00") | |
| [Ross Dependency](/wiki/Ross_Dependency "Ross Dependency") | [UTC+12:00](/wiki/UTC%2B12:00 "UTC+12:00") | [UTC+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") |

There are two [associated states](/wiki/Associated_states "Associated states") of New Zealand in the Pacific Ocean, both on the other side of the [International Date Line](/wiki/International_Date_Line "International Date Line"):

* The [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands") are in the [UTC−10:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9210:00 "UTC−10:00") time zone or [Military](/wiki/Military_time "Military time") W (Whiskey)[[1]](#cite_note-Military_Time_Zone_map-1) and do not observe daylight saving time. Clocks in the Cook Islands are 22 or 23 hours behind New Zealand: at noon on Thursday in New Zealand, it is 13:00 or 14:00 on Wednesday in the Cook Islands.
* [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue") is in the [UTC−11:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9211:00 "UTC−11:00") time zone or Military X (X-ray)[[1]](#cite_note-Military_Time_Zone_map-1) and does not observe daylight saving time. Clocks in Niue are 23 or 24 hours behind New Zealand: at noon on Thursday in New Zealand, it is noon or 1 pm on Wednesday in Niue.

The New Zealand dependency of [Tokelau](/wiki/Tokelau "Tokelau") is in the [UTC+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") time zone[[20]](#cite_note-20) or Military M' (Mike Prime)[[1]](#cite_note-Military_Time_Zone_map-1) and does not observe daylight saving time. Clocks in Tokelau are set to the same time as or 1 hour ahead of New Zealand: at noon on Thursday in New Zealand, it is 12:00 or 13:00 on Thursday in Tokelau.

The [Ross Dependency](/wiki/Ross_Dependency "Ross Dependency"), [McMurdo Station](/wiki/McMurdo_Station "McMurdo Station") and [Amundsen–Scott South Pole Station](/wiki/Amundsen%E2%80%93Scott_South_Pole_Station "Amundsen–Scott South Pole Station") in Antarctica maintain NZST/NZDT.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## IANA time zone database

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Time_in_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: IANA time zone database")]

The zones for New Zealand, its dependencies, and its associated states as given by [zone.tab](/wiki/Zone.tab "Zone.tab") of the [IANA time zone database](/wiki/IANA_time_zone_database "IANA time zone database").

| [c.c.](/wiki/ISO_3166_2-character_country_code "ISO 3166 2-character country code") | Coordinates | [Timezone name](/wiki/List_of_tz_database_time_zones "List of tz database time zones") | Comments | [UTC offset](/wiki/UTC_offset "UTC offset") ([Std.](/wiki/Standard_time "Standard time")) | UTC offset ([DST](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time")) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [NZ](/wiki/ISO_3166-1:NZ "ISO 3166-1:NZ") | −3652+17446 | Pacific/Auckland | New Zealand time | [+12:00](/wiki/UTC%2B12:00 "UTC+12:00") | [+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") |
| [NZ](/wiki/ISO_3166-1:NZ "ISO 3166-1:NZ") | −4357−17633 | Pacific/Chatham | Chatham Islands | [+12:45](/wiki/UTC%2B12:45 "UTC+12:45") | [+13:45](/wiki/UTC%2B13:45 "UTC+13:45") |
| [TK](/wiki/ISO_3166-1:TK "ISO 3166-1:TK") | −0922−17114 | Pacific/Fakaofo |  | [+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") | |
| [CK](/wiki/ISO_3166-1:CK "ISO 3166-1:CK") | −2114−15946 | Pacific/Rarotonga |  | [−10:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9210:00 "UTC−10:00") | |
| [NU](/wiki/ISO_3166-1:NU "ISO 3166-1:NU") | −1901−16955 | Pacific/Niue |  | [−11:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9211:00 "UTC−11:00") | |
| [AQ](/wiki/ISO_3166-1:AQ "ISO 3166-1:AQ") | −7750+16636 | Antarctica/McMurdo | New Zealand time – McMurdo, South Pole | [+12:00](/wiki/UTC%2B12:00 "UTC+12:00") | [+13:00](/wiki/UTC%2B13:00 "UTC+13:00") |
